coredata.dc:title: Novel Schiff base (DBDDP) selective detection of Fe (III): Dispersed in aqueous solution and encapsulated in silica cross-linked micellar nanoparticles in living cell
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Gai, Fangyuan
Yin, Li
Fan, Mengmeng
Li, Ling
Grahn, Johnny
Ao, Yuhui
Yang, Xudong
Wu, Xuming
Liu, Yunling
Huo, Qisheng
coredata.dc:description: This work demonstrated the synthesis of (4E)-4-(4-(diphenylamino)benzylideneamino)-1,2-dihydro-1,5- dimethyl-2-phenylpyrazol-3-one (DBDDP) for Fe (III) detection in aqueous media and in the core of silica cross-linked micellar nanoparticles in living cells. The free DBDDP performed fluorescence enhancement due to Fe (III)-promoted hydrolysis in a mixed aqueous solution, while the DBDDP-doped silica cross-linked micellar nanoparticles (DBDDP-SCMNPs) performed an electron-transfer based fluorescence quenching of Fe (III) in living cells. The quenching fluorescence of DBDDP-SCMNPs and the concentration of Fe (III) exhibited a linear correlation, which was in accordance with the Stern-Volmer equation. Moreover, DBDDP-SCMNPs showed a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 ppm and an excellent selectivity against other metal ions. Due to the good solubility and biocompatibility, DBDDP-SCMNPs could be applied as fluorescence quenching nanosensors in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasmall silica cross-linked micellar nanoparticles
Fluorecent sensing
Fe(III) selectivity

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe (III) by free DBDDP in aqueous media
Detection of Fe (III) by DBDDP-SCMNPs in aqueous media
Linear analysis and selectivity of Fe (III) sensing by DBDDP-SCMNPs

The authors would like to thank Prof. Huixuan Zhang, Prof. Changhai Liang and Prof. Ce Hao for their kind support. Mr. Xuming Wu would like to thank the support of Training Programs of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different solvent effects of fluorescent properties: (A) Fluorescent excitation spectra (black: CH3CN, red: C6H14, blue: THF and green: CH2Cl2), (B) Fluorescent emission spectra of the four samples and (C) P photo of different colour emission samples under ultraviolet.

(A) Fluorescent emission spectra of DBDDP in aqueous media, added with an increasing amount of FeCl3 from 0 to 1.6 ppm, (B) selectivity detection of Fe (III) by DBDDP in aqueous media, (C) visualized sensing ability of DBDDP and the selection of Fe (III) by the naked eye.
TEM image of DBDDP-SCMNPs (scale bar 100 nm) and the scheme structure of DBDDP-SCMNPs.

(A) Fluorescent excitation, emission and UV adsorption of DBDDP-SCMNPs, (B) DBDDP-SCMNPs added with an increasing amount of Fe(III) from 0.1 ppm to 1 ppm (The data of this figure have been measured 5 times).

(A) Linear correlation between the concentration of Fe (III) and the fluorescent intensity of DBDDP-SCMNPs, (B) DBDDP-SCMNPs added with different metal cations (The data of figure have been measured 5 times).
Confocal images of HeLa cells incubated with DBDDP-SCMNPs (A, B and C) and with DBDDP-SCMNPs and Fe(III) (D–F) for 5 h; (A, D) bright-field, (B. E) confocal fluorescent and (C, F) overlay images.

head.ce:title: Novel Schiff base (DBDDP) selective detection of Fe (III): Dispersed in aqueous solution and encapsulated in silica cross-linked micellar nanoparticles in living cell

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Biology, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Materials Science, Luleå University of Technology, SE-97187 Luleå, Sweden

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Biology, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, China (F. Gai).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work demonstrated that the design and synthesis of an electron transfer-based fluorescence quenching sensor using Silica cross-linked micellar nanoaparticles as scaffolds to encapsulate DBDDP for high selective detection of Fe3+ with a low limit of detection of 0.1 ppm.

This work demonstrated the synthesis of (4E)-4-(4-(diphenylamino)benzylideneamino)-1,2-dihydro-1,5- dimethyl-2-phenylpyrazol-3-one (DBDDP) for Fe (III) detection in aqueous media and in the core of silica cross-linked micellar nanoparticles in living cells. The free DBDDP performed fluorescence enhancement due to Fe (III)-promoted hydrolysis in a mixed aqueous solution, while the DBDDP-doped silica cross-linked micellar nanoparticles (DBDDP-SCMNPs) performed an electron-transfer based fluorescence quenching of Fe (III) in living cells. The quenching fluorescence of DBDDP-SCMNPs and the concentration of Fe (III) exhibited a linear correlation, which was in accordance with the Stern-Volmer equation. Moreover, DBDDP-SCMNPs showed a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 ppm and an excellent selectivity against other metal ions. Due to the good solubility and biocompatibility, DBDDP-SCMNPs could be applied as fluorescence quenching nanosensors in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of metal ions in aqueous media is important for human life, biological systems [1–3], and environmental science [4–8]. Among all the ions, iron is the most abundant transition metal ion, which can be easily found in rock and soil in the earth. Iron widely exists in biological systems as a trace element to chemical process of human body [9–12]. The ferrous/ferric (Fe2+/Fe3+) couples are the important redox states; both their deficiency and excess may induce many diseases and disorders of the human body [13,14]. For example, there will be a series of problems if the iron in the human body is less or more than the normal level [2,15,16]. On one hand, the excess of iron in the biological system will cause serious diseases [1,17]. On the other hand, the deficiency of iron may cause tissue damage and protein pathological changes to various organs of the human body [17,18]. Therefore, the detection of Fe2+ or Fe3+ with excellent selectivity and low limit of detection (LOD) is critical [2,19,20].

The considerable importance of iron in environmental systems has led to a large amount of attention in recent years [21–23]. The high selective determination techniques of iron for both qualitative and quantitative methods in biological, medical, environmental, and in different industrial samples have been developed at a fast rate. A variety of traditional detection techniques such as spectrophotometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, voltammetry, and total reflection X-ray fluorimetry have been used for the detection of iron in different media [1,2,16]. These techniques offer a good LOD and linear correlation. However, most of the above methods for iron detection were shown to be affected by other metal ions and the samples required complicated pre-treatment procedures, which will not fit for biological systems and online monitoring [17,24]. To avoid these detrimental problems, fluorescent techniques would be one of the best choices for online monitoring and intracellular sensing. These techniques could translate irons information into fluorescent signals [6,18,25].

Recently, various organic molecules have been designed and synthesized for iron detection. Duan and co-workers synthesized a series of Schiff base functionalized chitosan-derived fluorescent materials [6], which can not only detect Fe (III) in aqueous media but also absorb iron from water. Chelating organic molecules such as 1,8-Naphthalimide derivatives [26,27], rhodamine-based Schiff base [7,28], 2,3-dibromopentacenequinone derivatives, and coumarin derivatives have been introduced to the design and synthesis of iron fluorescent sensors [2]. However, the fluorescent sensor needs to be stable after dilution and the signal change should be as obvious as possible for use in biological applications [29,30].

As one of the effective strategies to develop Fe (III) fluorescent sensors for clinical diagnosis, it is common use to integrate organic sensing molecules with inorganic nanostructures [31–40]. including quantum dots [41], nanocrystals [42], nanoclusters [43,44], and silica nanoparticles [45–47], For example, Han et al. reported the fluorescence quenching nanosensor of Haeme (Fe (III)) by the use of functionalized CdTe quantum dots with different moieties in aqueous media [17]. Xin and co-workers developed novel Fe (III) by covalent grafting allylamine onto a graphene oxide surface [10]. By comparison with the above-mentioned scaffolds, silica cross-linked micellar nanoparticles (SCMNPs) exhibited a superior mesostructured hydrophobic core, which could be multifunctionalized with more organic and medical species for in vivo online monitoring and therapy. Furthermore, the organic molecule-doped SCMNPs [47–49] exhibited ultra-small sizes (∼15 nm), which are easier to be adapted and discharged out of the body than mesoporous silica nanoparticles in living cells. Our previous work introduced ultra-small silica cross-linked micellar nanoparticles (SCMNPs) as a scaffold to encapsulate organic molecules for constructing an Fe (III) sensing systems in aqueous media [24]. The LOD of Fe (III) could be decreased to 1 ppm by surface charge tuning of dye-doped SCMNPs [3].

In this study, we synthesized a Schiff based derivative, (4E)-4-(4-(diphenylamino) benzylideneamino)-1,2-dihydro- 1,5-dimethyl-2-phenylpyrazol-3-one (DBDDP), to integrate with the SCMNPs for constructing a highly selective and water-compatible Fe (III) nanosensor (DBDDP-SCMNPs). DBDDP-SCMNPs are capable of non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and water-compatibility when used in a living cell. The LOD of DBDDP-SCMNPs exhibited 0.1 ppm, which is much lower than that of the previous SCMNPs-based Fe (III) sensors. The quenching fluorescence of DBDDP-SCMNPs and the concentration of Fe (III) exhibited a superior linear correlation, which could be used to determine the quantity of Fe (III) both at low (0.1 ppm) and high (10 ppm) concentrations. Moreover, DBDDP-SCMNPs showed excellent biocompatibility and the ability to sense Fe (III) in a living cell.

4-formacyl-triphenylamine (99.99%) was purchased from Aladdin, Shanghai, China. Ethanol (CH3CH2OH, 95%), ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O, 99%), hydrochloric acid (fuming, ≥37%), ferricchlorideanhydrous (FeCl3, ≥97%), and acetonitrile (CH3CN, ≥97%) were purchased from Aladdin. 4-Aminoantipyrine (98%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Pluronic nonionic surfactant F127, diethoxydimethylsilane (Me2Si(OEt)2, DEDMS) and tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, 99.99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethanol (CH3CH2OH, 95%) hydrochloric acid (fuming, 37%), the other metal ions, and organic solvents were purchased from Aldrich. All reagents and solvents were used without further purification. Deionised water was used in the experiment (Resistivity ≥ 18.2 MΩ).

The synthetic route of DBDDP ((4E) -4-(4-(diphenylamino) benzylideneamino)-1,2-dihydro-1,5-dimethyl-2-phenylpyrazol-3-one) was shown in the Scheme 1 . The methods to synthesize the compound was based on our previous work [24]. The amount of 4-formacyl-triphenylamine and 4-aminoantipyrine could be weighed by a balance without further purification. In the mixture of 4-formacyl-triphenylamine, Ethanol, HOAc, and 4-aminoantipyrine, Schiff base DBDDP could be obtained as a yellow precipitation after refluxing for 3 h.

A total of 0.6 g of F127 and 9.4 g of HCl (0.85 M) were added to a glass vial. After stirring for 1 h, 1 g of TEOS was added to the above acidic solution. Then, 0.06 g of DEDMS was added to the vial after an additional 0.5 h of stirring. The as-prepared silica cross-linked micellar nanoparticles (SCMNPs) were collected after 12 h of stirring and dialyzed by deionised water to remove the HCl and other impurities. Three milligrams of DBDDP was dissolved in acetonitrile solvent to obtain its solution with a concentration of approximately 3.27 × 10−4 M. A specific amount of the DBDDP solution was put into a vial and the solvent was completely removed in a vacuum oven at 40 °C to prevent the organic solvent from destroying the micelles of F127. Then, 2 ml of the SCMNPs suspension was added to the vial. After 0.5 h of stirring, the DBDDP-SCMNPs were treated by dialysis (using a dialysis tube cut-off: 8000–14 400 and a 0.22 μm filter) to ensure that all the low molecular (such as HCl and silane) and large solid impurities were removed from the product.

The stability of DBDDP-SCMNPs could be detected by testing the fluorescence of DBDDP-SCMNPs and the dialysis water. After dialysis of as-synthesized DBDDP-SCMNPs, the excess of DBDDP in the core of SCMNPs would disperse into water out of the dialysis bag. The dialysis water has been condensed by boiling off some water until the volume is equal to that of DBDDP-SCMNPs. Then, the fluorescent spectra of the condensed dialysis water and DBDDP-SCMNPs have been tested to show that almost no fluorescent signal could be detected in the dialysis water (Fig. S7), suggesting almost no leakage of the DBDDP could be found.

The HeLa cells (purchased from Lonza) were cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, Lonza), supplemented with 10% Foetal Bovine Serum (FBS; Lonza) 1% penicillin/streptomycin. Cells were plated in WillCo Wells and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. For the experiments with the addition of Fe(III), a parallel series of samples was incubated for 12 additional hours with FeCl3 supplemented at 37 °C and 5% CO2 of DMEM. The FeCl3 concentrations tested were 20, 40, 80 and 120 µM. Before the experiments, cells were washed with a PBS buffer and incubated 5 min at 25 °C with the probe (DBDDP-SCMNPs) solutions. Suspensions (15 μg/ml) of DBDDP-SCMNPs from the stock solution were prepared with the serum-containing DMEM. 10 × 104 cells were seeded in a 6-well cell culture plate within the cell culture medium. On the treatment day, the culture medium was replaced with 200 μl of the medium containing 30 μl/ml of DBDDP-SCMNPs. The cells were then incubated at 37 °C for 5 h. The excess DBDDP-SCMNPs were removed by washing 3 times with warm phosphate buffered saline (PBS).

The fluorescence emission spectra and 3D spectra of the samples were recorded on a HORIBA Instrument Fluoro Max- Compact spectrofluorometer. 1H NMR of DBDDP was obtained on an Advance III instrument (Brucker, 300 MHz). Mass spectrometry (MS) of DBDDP was carried out on a Brucker Instrument (Agilent 1290-micr OTOF Q II). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using an FEI Tecnal G2 F20 s-twin D573 operating at 200 kV. The samples for TEM were prepared by directly placing a small drop of the nanoparticle suspension on a carbon-coated TEM grid. The particle size of DBDDP-SCMNPs was measured by using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-S instrument using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) principles with a HeNe laser (633 nm). The confocal microscopy images were taken with an Olympus Fluoview FV1000. All measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions.

To design and synthesize a different chemosensor for encapsulating in the core of SCMNPs, we chose triphenylamine as a receptor moiety, which performed blue light emission under ultraviolet light and its stereotypical structure tends to be a twist [50]. Our basic idea is to construct a strong stereotypical effect of the Schiff based-triphenylamine for a unique array in the core of SCMNPs. Moreover, due to the conformational twist of the Schiff base (DBDDP), DBDDP-SCMNPs may exhibit a fantastic optical process.

We first investigated the different solvent effects of the DBDDP fluorescent properties. As shown in Fig. 1 , we can find that the different polarity solvent can cause different fluorescent emissions of the samples under the radiation of ultraviolet light. Fig. 1A suggested the different fluorescent excitations of samples when we dissolved a certain amount of DBDDP in the solvent of THF, CH3CN, C6H14 and CH2Cl2 at the same concentration. The delicate structure of the DBDDP excitation spectra has been observed with two peaks located at 330 nm and 400 nm. However, the THF C6H14 and CH2Cl2 will not cause two peaks of fluorescent excitation spectra. Similar shapes and results can be detected in the fluorescent emission spectra, which demonstrated a positive correlation with the excitation spectra, as shown in Fig. 1B. We could find that except CH3CN, the other three solvents will not cause a delicate structure on the fluorescent emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 1C, when DBDDP is dissolved in different organic solutions, different colour emissions would be detected under UV light. From left to right, the DBDDP has been dissolved in the solvent of CH3CN, CH2Cl2, THF, and C6H14. The colour of the samples is listed according to the fluorescent emission spectra (Fig. 1B). This result is according to the previous literature of D-π-A molecule [51]. The fluorescent emission spectra of DBDDP dissolved in different solvent showed a colour-tunable fluorescence in the solvents with different polarity in accordance with the occurrence of the conformational twist.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe (III) by free DBDDP in aqueous media
ce:section.ce:para: DBDDP is a Schiff based molecule, where a CN bond could hydrolyse by using a Lewis acid such as Fe (III) and other metal irons. The degree of hydrolysis and the fluorescence change is related to the different type of Lewis acids, which may cause further reactions with the CN bond [50]. As shown in Fig. 2 (A), by adding 0.2 ppm of FeCl3 into the DBDDP solution, the fluorescent spectra intensity of DBDDP gradually enhanced from 1260 to 269,410. The shape of the fluorescent emission spectra changed and shifted from 440 nm to 550 nm, which was induced by the aldehyde formation during hydrolysis [24]. This has been confirmed by the comparison of molecule weight before and after adding a certain amount of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S1, the molecule weight of DBDDP is 459, while DBDDP added with Fe3+ performed with a molecule weight of 274. This result relates to the metal-promoted hydrolysis of the Schiff base bond of DBDDP, as shown in Fig. S2.

We investigated the selectivity of DBDDP by testing the fluorescence of free DBDDP by adding different metal ions in aqueous media. As shown in Fig. 2(B), the same amount of metal ions (Al3+, Co2+, Cu+, Sc3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ni2+ and Zn2+) have been added to the DBDDP solution at the concentration of 10−5 M. We found that DBDDP enhanced the redshifted fluorescence after we added Al3+, Co2+, Cu+, Sc3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ni2+ and Zn2+ to the solution. However, the most obvious increase of intensity and redshift is caused by Fe (III). As shown in Fig. 2(C), by adding a certain amount of Al3+, Co2+, Cu+, Fe2+, Ni2+ and Zn2+ into the DBDDP solution, no colour changes have been detected by the naked eyes. However, the addition of Fe (III) induced an obvious colour change of the DBDDP-SCMNPs, which may result from the formation of complexes by Fe (III) and the hydrolysis product.

To introduce DBDDP into the core of SCMNPs, we used the encapsulate method [52]. The FTIR, XRD and EDS of DBDDP-SCMNPs results are similar to our previous work (see Supporting information) [53], indicating that the stable DBDDP-SCMNPs have been synthesized during the encapsulation process. The basic idea of our synthesis process is to allow the polymeric surfactant to self-assemble into micellar structures under acidic conditions [52]. The micelle of F127 possessed a hydrophobic core and hydrophilic tails, which would induce DBDDP to accumulate in the hydrophobic core. After that, the hydrolysis and polymerization of silicate would form at the edge of the interface between the hydrophobic core and the hydrophilic tail [3,24,53]. As shown in Fig. 3 , DBDDP-SCMNPs displayed dispersity, uniformity, and a spherical shape in the TEM image. The diameter of DBDDP-SCMNPs is approximately 15 nm, which is according to the literature [53]. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique suggested the PDI value of DBDDP-SCMNPs is lower than 0.1, indicating excellent monodispersity of the nanoparticles in the aqueous solution [53]. However, the diameter of DBDDP-SCMNPs tested by the DLS technique is approximately 24.7 nm, which is larger than the diameter tested by TEM. This larger diameter is ascribed to the hydrophilic tails of DBDDP-SCMNPs in the aqueous media. This result is similar to other dye-doped SCMNPs in our previous work [3,24,52,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe (III) by DBDDP-SCMNPs in aqueous media
ce:section.ce:para: Our original idea is to encapsulate DBDDP in SCMNPs for constructing unique optical properties of the DBDDP in the confined hydrophobic core. The quantum yield of DBDDP-SCMNPs is 0.67, while free DBDDP dissolved in THF exhibits a quantum yield of 0.64. This result suggested that the optical efficiency of DBDDP-SCMNPs is similar to the free DBDDP in the solution. As we reported before, the SCMNPs would induce D-π-A molecule assembly in the confined hydrophobic core [24]. Thus, the electron transfer process from DBDDP-SCMNPs to Fe (III) would cause fluorescence quenching of DBDDP-SCMNPs. As shown in Fig. 4 (A), the fluorescent excitation and emission peak of DBDDP-SCMNPs was located at 348 nm and 474 nm, respectively. The UV adsorption peak of DBDDP-SCMNPs was located at 374 nm. The shape and intensity of DBDDP-SCMNPs fluorescence spectra are similar to the free DBDDP solution (Fig. S5), which suggested that the silica shell would not affect the fluorescent emission of the DBDDP in the core of the micelle. As shown in Fig. 4(B), by adding a certain amount of Fe (III) into the DBDDP-SCMNPs aqueous solution, we could find that the fluorescent intensity of DBDDP-SCMNPs quenched gradually. With the addition of Fe (III) increasing continually, DBDDP-SCMNPs fluorescence quenched to 5% of their original intensity. The ζ potential of DBDDP-SCMNPs is −16.3 mV, indicating the surface of the particle is negatively charged. The negative-charged surface of DBDDP-SCMNPs would absorb Fe (III) onto the shell of the particles [3], in which the distance between Fe (III) and DBDDP-SCMNPs would be narrowed down and fit for electron transfer. Moreover, the quenching process has not changed the shape and location of the fluorescent spectra, which gave more evidence of the electron transfer process from DBDDP to Fe (III) in the DBDDP-SCMNPs sensing system. The LOD of Fe (III) is 0.1 ppm, which is much lower than that of the previous SCMNPs-based Fe (III) sensors [3,24]. This may be attributed to the unique array of DBDDP in the confined core of SCMNPs, which has a conformational twist of DBDDP that facilitated the electron transfer-based Fe (III) detection. The higher concentration of Fe (III) (From 1 ppm to 10 ppm) could be detected by DBDDP-SCMNPs contained with more DBDDP. Thus, DBDDP-SCMNPs could test both diluted and condensed samples of Fe (III) (Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear analysis and selectivity of Fe (III) sensing by DBDDP-SCMNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Further investigation of fluorescence quenching by Fe (III) had been conducted using the Stern–Volmer equation: I0/I = 1 + KSV [Q], where I0 is the original fluorescence intensity in the absence of Fe (III), I is the fluorescence intensity of DBDDP-SCMNPs with different concentrations of Fe (III), KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, and Q is the concentration of the quencher. Thus, the concentration of the quencher could be calculated by the decrease in fluorescent intensity of the sensor. From the observation of Fig. 5 (A), we found that when the concentration of Fe (III) is between 0.1 ppm and 2.1 ppm, the linear correlation is obvious. The 0.1 ppm of LOD is much lower than the iron concentration in and blood of patients with melanoma [54] and the upper concentration in drinking water [55], suggesting that the measurement range of DBDDP-SCMNPs is in a range appropriate for biological and environmental samples. Fig. 5(B) presented a corresponding linear equation of Y = 2.30108–2.24376 X, where Y is the relative fluorescent intensity (I/I0), X is the concentration of Fe (III) in the DBDDP-SCMNPs sensing system, and R is the correlation coefficient. We could use this equation for the calculation of Fe (III) concentrations in a range from 0.1 ppm to 2.5 ppm. Fig. S6 suggested that the higher concentration of Fe (III) and the quenching of DBDDP-SCMNPs also had a linear correlation, which could also be used to calculate Fe (III) concentration with a specific quantity (from 1 to 10 ppm). Thus, DBDDP-SCMNPs could be adjusted to detect Fe (III) in a specific quantity with both high and low concentration. This process has more extensive application prospects by comparison than other SCMNPs-based sensors [3,24,53].

Subsequently, the selectivity of Fe (III) has been investigated by testing the metal ions influence on the DBDDP-SCMNPs fluorescent emission, as shown in Fig. 5(B). We found that almost no obvious fluorescent quenching could be detected after the addition of Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+. Conversely, the same concentration of Fe (III) caused obvious fluorescent quenching, indicating an excellent selective ability for DBDDP-SCMNPs.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the HeLa cells incubated with DBDDP-SCMNPs have been tested by confocal microscopy. The strong blue light emission of the HeLa cell incubated with DBDDP-SCMNPs could be detected under UV light radiation (Fig. 6B), which suggests that the DBDDP-SCMNPs exhibited excellent compatibility within the HeLa cell. By characterizing the sensing ability of DBDDP-SCMNPs in HeLa cells, we investigated the fluorescent emission of a HeLa cell incubated with DBDDP-SCMNPs towards the intracellular sensing of Fe3+ species. The fluorescence performance of the HeLa cell was demonstrated in Fig. 6E, where the HeLa cell was incubated with DBDDP-SCMNPs and Fe3+ (4 ppm), which showed weaker blue light emission by comparison with the sample that was incubated without DBDDP-SCMNPs (Fig. 6B). Therefore, DBDDP-SCMNPs could be applied to cell sensing and imaging for Fe (III) detection.

In summary, we designed and synthesized an electron transfer-based fluorescence quenching nanosensor using SCMNPs as scaffolds to encapsulate DBDDP. For medical applications, DBDDP-SCMNPs exhibited a superior meso-structured hydrophobic core and ultra-small size, which could be more stable and easier to use by comparison with the myriad of nonporous nanosensors [1,10,13,14,17,56]. Moreover, DBDDP-SCMNPs showed a much lower Fe (III) LOD when compared to the previous SCMNPs-based Fe (III) sensors [3,24]. DBDDP-SCMNPs could be adjusted to detect Fe (III) in a specific quantity with both low concentration (0.1 ppm–2.5 ppm) and high concentration (from 1 to 10 ppm), which could be more extensively applied in fields other than SCMNPs-based sensors [3,24,53,57]. Therefore, stable, water-compatible, and biocompatible DBDDP-SCMNPs possess potential applications for the accurate detection of Fe (III) in living cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21501018 and 51603018) and the Open project of State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry (No. 2018-29). The authors would like to thank Prof. Huixuan Zhang, Prof. Changhai Liang and Prof. Ce Hao for their kind support. Mr. Xuming Wu would like to thank the support of Training Programs of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.12.050.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: E.G. Jaramillo, E. Mupere, R.O. Opoka, J.S. Hodges, T.C. Lund, M.K. Georgieff, C.C. John, S.E. Cusick, in: PLoS One, 2017, pp. e0183977.
Y.-P. Z. Ali Md Showkat, Min Seok Kim, Anantha Iyengar Gopalan, Kakarla Raghava Reddy, Kwang-Pill Lee, Bull. Kor. Chem. Soc., 28 (2007) 1985–1992.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Polym. Sci. Part A: Polym. Chem.
